Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd put in maybe four or five hours a day across the week, but it wasn't really structured or anything. I'd listen to lectures while doing other stuff around the house, or I'd have podcasts and audio materials playing in the background. It felt like I was doing something, but looking back, I wasn't really absorbing much. I just kind of drifted through it without a real plan.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, but not really because of deadlines—more because I just couldn't get myself to care enough to stick to a schedule. Some weeks I'd be more active, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until I remembered an assignment was due. I'm 24 and I've been at this university thing for a while now, so the urgency kind of faded. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't often enough apparently.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got a notification about something or I remembered there was an assignment coming up. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit, scroll around for a bit, then get bored and leave. It wasn't like I was excited to be there or anything. The mindfulness stuff they had us do actually helped me be more aware of when I was just going through the motions versus actually engaging.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became kind of automatic after a while. I'd do it while eating breakfast or during downtime, just checking if anything new was posted. But routine doesn't mean I was really present mentally. I was going through the motions most of the time. It was easy to click around and feel like I was being productive without actually learning anything substantial.
Q5: The audio lectures and video content were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd play them while doing chores or lying in bed, which made it feel less like work. The discussion boards were okay too—sometimes hearing other students explain things in their own words clicked better than the official materials. But even then, I wasn't always paying full attention.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the dense reading materials and anything that required me to sit down and focus for long periods. My resources are limited—no internet at home, so I'd have to do everything on campus or at the library. That made it harder to engage with stuff that needed constant online access. If something looked boring or complicated, I'd just skip it and hope it wouldn't matter.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was quick, easy to understand, and I could do it while half-distracted. If I could listen to it or if it had clear, immediate relevance to an assignment, I'd actually engage. Anything that felt like busywork or required deep concentration just made me zone out. I know that's not great, but I couldn't force myself to care about stuff that felt pointless.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Like, if it wasn't going to affect my grade, why bother? Required stuff I'd at least attempt, even if I wasn't fully into it. The optional materials might have been helpful, but I never had enough motivation to explore them. There's a big difference between 'should do' and 'have to do' when you're already struggling to stay interested.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download audio files and lectures to my phone since I don't have internet at home. Then I'd listen to them on the bus or while walking around. I also had some notes from classmates that I'd look over. A lot of my studying happened offline, just listening to stuff on repeat hoping it would sink in. The LMS wouldn't show any of that.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was technically studying but the LMS wouldn't know. Like I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times over the week while doing other things. Or I'd be talking through concepts with a friend from class at a café. None of that shows up in the system, but it was still studying—just not the kind that leaves a digital footprint.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd access something once to download it, then never open it on the LMS again. My whole strategy was to grab what I needed and take it offline. Without reliable internet, that was the only way I could study consistently. So my login activity probably looks way lower than the actual time I spent with the materials.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't engage. I'd feel bored and disconnected, like none of it mattered. Sometimes life stuff would get in the way—work, personal issues, just general exhaustion from being a 24-year-old who's been in school too long. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me notice when I was checking out, but noticing didn't always mean I could fix it.
Q13: Yeah, fatigue was a big one. I'd be tired from other responsibilities and just not have the mental energy for coursework. When I was confused about something, I'd avoid it rather than push through. The lack of internet at home meant I had to plan around when I could access things, which added another layer of hassle. It all piled up and made it easier to just not bother.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have videos playing but I'd be thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. I was physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out. My engagement numbers probably look decent, but the quality of that engagement was pretty low. I was just filling time, not actually learning.
Q15: I postponed exam prep more than anything else. I knew it was important but I just couldn't make myself care enough to really study for it. Assignments I could handle because they were concrete tasks, but studying for exams felt overwhelming and pointless. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I'd just drift and do nothing productive. When something was due, I'd suddenly become active and get it done, usually at the last minute. The LMS probably shows spikes of activity right before due dates. That's just how I operated—crisis mode was my default study mode.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. I'd read it, think 'okay, that makes sense,' and then continue doing the same things. It wasn't that the feedback was bad—I just didn't have the motivation to actually apply it. Good feedback might make me feel momentarily better, bad feedback would bum me out, but neither really changed my behavior in a lasting way.
Q18: The course structure was fine, I guess. It was organized enough that I could figure out what needed to be done and when. But the structure didn't make me want to engage more—it just made it easier to do the minimum. Having clear deadlines helped, but having flexibility also meant I could procrastinate without immediate consequences. It was a double-edged sword.
Q19: The video content and audio options made engagement easier for someone like me who learns by listening. But the heavy reading requirements and anything that needed constant online access made things harder. Not having internet at home was a real barrier that the course design didn't really account for. If more stuff had been downloadable or available offline, I might have engaged more.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happened to me. I was logging in, watching videos, posting in discussions, completing tasks—but when it came to actually understanding the material deeply enough for exams, I hadn't done the work. Activity doesn't equal learning. You can be busy without being productive. I was going through motions without the substance behind it.
Q21: Yeah, I've known people who barely touched the LMS but did well because they studied from textbooks or had study groups outside the system. If you're efficient and focused, you don't need to spend hours clicking around online. The LMS is just one tool. Some people learn better without it, and their grades reflect their actual understanding, not their login frequency.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you spent on pages. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or just had it open in the background. They miss offline studying, quality of engagement, and whether anything actually stuck in your brain. They measure presence, not learning. There's a big difference.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real mental effort. It's not just showing up or completing tasks—it's being present and invested. For me, I was rarely truly engaged this semester. I was going through the motions, checking boxes, but my mind was somewhere else. Real engagement is something I struggled to achieve.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how disconnected and bored I felt most of the time. It wouldn't show that I was dealing with low motivation and just trying to get through the semester. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did or the fact that my high activity numbers didn't translate to actual understanding. The data makes me look more engaged than I actually was, and it completely misses the mental and emotional side of things.
